project_goal: Create a mobile-first application that identifies butterflies and moths from camera or gallery photos entirely on-device and lets casual users build a private personal collection, while preserving the completed car-category foundations for a later category pack.
target_users: Casual users and nature enthusiasts who are curious about butterflies and moths they encounter and want to collect identified sightings

mvp.summary: An Android-first offline butterfly and moth categorizer and personal album initially covering the 162 Austrian species or inseparable species pairs in the accepted dataset that have at least 50 expert-validated images.
must_have: Accept both camera captures and gallery imports.
Run recognition fully on-device with a model bundled in the application.
Identify the supported butterfly or moth species, using a combined label for species pairs that cannot be distinguished reliably from photographs.
Present ranked recognition candidates and allow manual correction when none is correct.
Support the 162 dataset classes with at least 50 expert-validated images, subject to independent license, class-map, mobile-runtime, and held-out evaluation gates.
Store app-managed photo copies with unnecessary metadata removed.
Maintain a local-only album containing the photo, confirmed identity, date, favorite state, and personal notes.
Provide manual export and import for backup or transfer.
postponed: Car recognition and the already prepared 200-class car catalog
Butterfly and moth species outside the accepted Austrian dataset
Location tracking, statistics, badges, and gamification
Telemetry and user-photo uploads for model training

target_platforms: Android 10 or newer on recent mid-range devices with approximately 4 GB RAM or more

stack.preference: Kotlin Multiplatform with Compose Multiplatform for the application, ONNX Runtime for on-device inference, and Python with PyTorch and TorchVision for model development and ONNX export.
stack.recommendation: Use shared Kotlin domain and UI foundations while keeping camera, storage, and inference integrations behind platform-specific adapters; validate every exported ONNX model against the Android runtime.

options.name: Kotlin Multiplatform, Compose Multiplatform, and ONNX Runtime
options.best_for: Android-first delivery with a low-refactor path to iOS and desktop
risks: Camera, storage, and inference require platform-specific adapters.
ONNX operator compatibility and mobile performance must be verified for every model export.

options.name: Native Android with Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, and LiteRT
risks: Later iOS and desktop applications would require substantial additional platform work.

options.name: Flutter with platform-specific ONNX Runtime integrations
options.best_for: A shared Dart UI across mobile and desktop
risks: Inference integration crosses Dart and native boundaries on each platform.

notes: Requirements and bootstrap work is prepared on assembly/bootstrap-categorizer.

tools.name: Android Studio or IntelliJ IDEA
tools.configuration: Kotlin Multiplatform and Android development support
tools.notes: Exact local installation and version constraints will be resolved during planning.

tools.name: Python ML environment
tools.configuration: PyTorch, TorchVision, ONNX export, and ONNX Runtime verification
tools.notes: Dependencies must be pinned after the initial licensed dataset and baseline model are selected.

team_mode.parallelization_goal: Coordinate two or three AI agents across independently scoped application, ML pipeline, and validation work.

working_style.review_style: Use focused pull requests with explicit contracts between application, model pipeline, and validation work.
proof_required: Relevant automated tests and static checks pass.
The exported ONNX model produces equivalent ranked outputs in the training environment and Android runtime within documented tolerances.
Held-out evaluation reports top-five accuracy and per-class results across the supported catalog.
A selected mid-range Android device reports median inference latency from a reproducible benchmark.
Closed-track installation, offline recognition, album persistence, export, and import are demonstrated.

constraints: Recognition must not require an LLM, subscription, remote inference service, or network connection.
The bundled model and application must operate within the storage, memory, and performance limits of supported Android devices.
Training datasets, pretrained weights, and other assets must have licenses compatible with the intended application and distribution.
The Figshare dataset record and every consumed artifact must retain its CC BY 4.0 attribution and provenance record.
The supported MVP catalog is limited to the 162 sufficiently represented Austrian Lepidoptera classes used by the published baseline.
The architecture should preserve practical portability to iOS and desktop without adding those deliverables to the MVP.
safety_boundaries: Keep collection data and inference local to the device in the MVP.
Remove unnecessary image metadata, including location metadata, from app-managed copies.
Do not upload user photos, corrections, or collection data for training or analytics.
Do not collect accounts, credentials, or cloud data for the MVP.
Do not use datasets, model weights, or images without verified compatible licensing.
assumptions: Scientific species names are canonical labels; common names may be added as display metadata without changing class identity.
Four visually inseparable species pairs remain combined exactly as defined by the source dataset.
Manual correction is stored as album metadata and is not used for automatic retraining in the MVP.
The app may report that a car is unsupported or uncertain instead of forcing a confident identification.
Manual export and import use a documented, versioned format suitable for later migration or synchronization.
Development builds may be used before the required Google Play closed-testing milestone.

open_questions.question: Does the provided MaxViT-T checkpoint satisfy artifact-specific provenance, ONNX compatibility, size, memory, and latency gates, or must it be distilled into a smaller mobile model?
open_questions.risk_if_unanswered: The accepted dataset is usable, but the release model and amount of source-image acquisition remain unresolved.

open_questions.question: Which specific mid-range Android device will be the latency benchmark target?
open_questions.risk_if_unanswered: The accepted two-second target lacks a reproducible hardware reference.

open_questions.question: What maximum bundled application and model size is acceptable for closed testing?
open_questions.risk_if_unanswered: Model selection and quantization cannot be bounded by a release-size budget.

acceptance_signals: A held-out representative test set shows the correct supported Lepidoptera class within the top five candidates at least 80 percent of the time.
Per-class evaluation is reported so common classes cannot conceal weak supported classes.
Median on-device inference is below two seconds on the selected mid-range Android benchmark device.
Camera and gallery photos of supported butterflies and moths can be classified without network access, confirmed or corrected, and saved to the album.
Album entries survive application restarts and can be searched, filtered, favorited, annotated, exported, and imported.
Stored app-managed photos omit unnecessary metadata.
A signed build containing the bundled model is installable through Google Play closed testing.
